questions.question: What is the first goal of a consistency drill answer?
options: Clarify which flows need what guarantees before choosing replication, transactions, or workflow patterns.
Choose the strongest database mode available immediately.
Avoid talking about failures so more time remains for diagrams.
Assume every entity needs the same consistency level.
questions.explanation: The drill rewards scoped guarantees, not blanket slogans.

questions.question: Why should the drill force explicit degraded-mode behavior?
options: Because lag, failover, and partial workflow failure change what users can safely observe, and the design must say what happens then.
Because strong consistency removes the need for any fallback.
Because degraded mode only matters for caching lessons.
Because production systems can hide those choices from operators.
questions.explanation: Senior answers define behavior under failure, not just the happy path.

questions.question: Which answer sounds strongest?
options: I will use strong consistency everywhere and revisit details if the interviewer asks.
I will map guarantees by entity, choose selective replication and transaction boundaries, and close with lag, contention, and compensation behavior.
I will start with a quorum formula and hope the rest is implied.
I will avoid workflow design because consistency and transactions are enough.
questions.explanation: That answer ties the design to concrete guarantees and operating behavior.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of one uniform consistency rule for all paths?
options: You usually overspend on coordination for cheap paths or underprotect critical ones because different entities need different guarantees.
It always reduces latency for every operation.
It removes the need for observability tiers.
It guarantees hotspot elimination.

questions.question: Which metric pair best connects internal correctness behavior to user risk?
options: Freshness mismatch or version skew together with critical-flow latency or completion success.
Only total number of repositories and packages.
Average note-taking time and quiz count.
The number of build lessons in the phase.
questions.explanation: That pair ties system state to user impact.

questions.question: What trade-off often appears when you shrink a transaction boundary?
options: You reduce synchronous contention and blast radius, but you must coordinate later work with idempotency and compensation.
You automatically eliminate all retries.
You make replication lag irrelevant.
You no longer need workflow state.
questions.explanation: Smaller transactional scope often pushes complexity into async coordination.

questions.question: Why should hotspot analysis appear even in a high-level consistency drill?
options: Because one key or entity can dominate write contention and make the chosen consistency model fail operationally before the rest of the design matters.
Because hotspots only affect rate limiters.
Because quorum overlap removes skew automatically.
Because hot keys matter only for caching.
questions.explanation: Consistency choices must survive concentrated traffic, not just average traffic.

questions.question: What should usually come last in a strong drill answer?
options: A concise wrap-up on guarantees, failure handling, observability, and the main trade-offs of the chosen coordination model.
A long list of unrelated database brands.
An apology for not proving every theorem.
A claim that exact time ordering solves the remaining issues.
questions.explanation: Senior answers close by making operational and trade-off implications explicit.
